title: Mesmerism
content:
I.
آ آ آ آ All I believed is true!
آ آ آ آ I am able yet
آ آ آ آ All I want, to get
آ آ آ آ By a method as strange as new:
آ آ آ آ Dare I trust the same to you?

II.
آ آ آ آ If at night, when doors are shut,
آ آ آ آ And the wood-worm picks,
آ آ آ آ And the death-watch ticks,
آ آ آ آ And the bar has a flag of smut,
آ آ آ آ And a catï؟½s in the water-butt,

III.
آ آ آ آ And the socket floats and flares,
آ آ آ آ And the house-beams groan,
آ آ آ آ And a foot unknown
آ آ آ آ Is surmised on the garret-stairs,
آ آ آ آ And the locks slip unawares,

IV.
آ آ آ آ And the spider, to serve his ends,
آ آ آ آ By a sudden thread,
آ آ آ آ Arms and legs outspread,
آ آ آ آ On the tableï؟½s midst descends,
آ آ آ آ Comes to find, God knows what friends!

V.
آ آ آ آ If since eve drew in, I say,
آ آ آ آ I have sat and brought
آ آ آ آ (So to speak) my thought
آ آ آ آ To bear on the woman away,
آ آ آ آ Till I felt my hair turn grey.

VI.
آ آ آ آ Till I seemed to have and hold,
آ آ آ آ In the vacancy
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twixt the wall and me,
آ آ آ آ From the hair-plaitï؟½s chestnut gold
آ آ آ آ To the foot in its muslin fold.

VII.
آ آ آ آ Have and hold, then and there,
آ آ آ آ Her, from head to foot,
آ آ آ آ Breathing and mute,
آ آ آ آ Passive and yet aware,
آ آ آ آ In the grasp of my steady stare.

VIII.
آ آ آ آ Hold and have, there and then,
آ آ آ آ All her body and soul
آ آ آ آ That completes my Whole,
آ آ آ آ All that women add to men,
آ آ آ آ In the clutch of my steady ken.

IX.
آ آ آ آ Having and holding, till
آ آ آ آ I imprint her fast
آ آ آ آ On the void at last
آ آ آ آ As the sun does whom he will
آ آ آ آ By the calotypistï؟½s skill.

X.
آ آ آ آ Then, if my heartï؟½s strength serve,
آ آ آ آ And through all and each
آ آ آ آ Of the veils I reach
آ آ آ آ To her soul and never swerve,
آ آ آ آ Knitting an iron nerve.

XI.
آ آ آ آ Command her soul to advance
آ آ آ آ And inform the shape
آ آ آ آ Which has made escape
آ آ آ آ And before my countenance
آ آ آ آ Answers me glance for glance.

XII.
آ آ آ آ I, still with a gesture fit
آ آ آ آ Of my hands that best
آ آ آ آ Do my soulï؟½s behest,
آ آ آ آ Pointing the power from it,
آ آ آ آ While myself do steadfast sit.

XIII.
آ آ آ آ Steadfast and still the same
آ آ آ آ On my object bent,
آ آ آ آ While the hands give vent
آ آ آ آ To my ardour and my aim
آ آ آ آ And break into very flame.

XIV.
آ آ آ آ Then I reach, I must believe,
آ آ آ آ Not her soul in vain,
آ آ آ آ For to me again
آ آ آ آ It reaches, and past retrieve
آ آ آ آ Is wound in the toils I weave.

XV.
آ آ آ آ And must follow as I require,
آ آ آ آ As befits a thrall,
آ آ آ آ Bringing flesh and all,
آ آ آ آ Essence and earth-attire,
آ آ آ آ To the source of the tractile fire.

XVI.
آ آ آ آ Till the house called hers, not mine,
آ آ آ آ With a growing weight
آ آ آ آ Seems to suffocate
آ آ آ آ If she break not its leaden line
آ آ آ آ And escape from its close confine.

XVII.
آ آ آ آ Out of doors into the night!
آ آ آ آ On to the maze
آ آ آ آ Of the wild wood-ways,
آ آ آ آ Not turning to left nor right
آ آ آ آ From the pathway, blind with sight.

XVIII.
آ آ آ آ Making throï؟½ rain and wind
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er the broken shrubs,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twixt the stems and stubs,
آ آ آ آ With a still, composed, strong mind,
آ آ آ آ Nor a care for the world behind.

XIX.
آ آ آ آ Swifter and still more swift,
آ آ آ آ As the crowding peace
آ آ آ آ Doth to joy increase
آ آ آ آ In the wide blind eyes uplift
آ آ آ آ Throï؟½ the darkness and the drift!

XX.
آ آ آ آ While I, to the shape, I too
آ آ آ آ Feel my soul dilate
آ آ آ آ Nor a whit abate,
آ آ آ آ And relax not a gesture due,
آ آ آ آ As I see my belief come true.

XXI.
آ آ آ آ For, there! have I drawn or no
آ آ آ آ Life to that lip?
آ آ آ آ Do my fingers dip
آ آ آ آ In a flame which again they throw
آ آ آ آ On the cheek that breaks a-glow?

XXII.
آ آ آ آ Ha! was the hair so first?
آ آ آ آ What, unfilleted,
آ آ آ آ Made alive, and spread
آ آ آ آ Through the void with a rich outburst,
آ آ آ آ Chestnut gold-interspersed!

XXTII.
آ آ آ آ Like the doors of a casket-shrine,
آ آ آ آ See, on either side,
آ آ آ آ Her two arms divide
آ آ آ آ Till the heart betwixt makes sign,
آ آ آ آ Take me, for I am thine!

XXIV.
آ آ آ آ Now, now, the door is heard!
آ آ آ آ Hark, the stairs! and near,
آ آ آ آ Nearer, and here,
آ آ آ آ Now! and at call the third
آ آ آ آ She enters without a word.

XXV.
آ آ آ آ On doth she march and on
آ آ آ آ To the fancied shape,
آ آ آ آ It is, past escape,
آ آ آ آ Herself, now, the dream is done
آ آ آ آ And the shadow and she are one.

XXVI.
آ آ آ آ First I will pray. Do Thou
آ آ آ آ That ownest the soul,
آ آ آ آ Yet wilt grant control
آ آ آ آ To another, nor disallow
آ آ آ آ For a time, restrain me now!

XXVII.
آ آ آ آ I admonish me while I may,
آ آ آ آ Not to squander guilt,
آ آ آ آ Since require Thou wilt
آ آ آ آ At my hand its price one day
آ آ آ آ What the price is, who can say?
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
